sourceMovie.title: Drag Me to Hell
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Drag Me to Hell (2009) stuck because it commits to its horror premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Sam Raimi's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The horror and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Ninth Gate
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Ninth Gate (1999) is the kind of film Drag Me to Hell fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The horror overlap is real, but it's the dark emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Candyman
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Candyman shares Drag Me to Hell's gift for horror — the 1992 film delivers thriller without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Inland Empire
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Drag Me to Hell compelling — the horror, the thriller — shows up in Inland Empire (2006) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: The Entity
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror energy of Drag Me to Hell is alive in The Entity (1982), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Behind the Mask: The Rise of Leslie Vernon
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Behind the Mask: The Rise of Leslie Vernon (2006) operates in the same horror as Drag Me to Hell, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: The Cursed
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Cursed (2021) belongs to the same conversation as Drag Me to Hell — both anchor their horror in thriller emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Ghost
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Drag Me to Hell's thriller stayed with you, Ghost (1990) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The fantasy through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Child's Play
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Drag Me to Hell excels in horror, Child's Play (1988) doubles down on craft to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: The Voices
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Voices (2014) is the natural next step after Drag Me to Hell: same horror DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and comedy elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: The Frighteners
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Drag Me to Hell who respond to its horror will find The Frighteners (1996) immediately familiar. Both films share a comedy sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

faq.question: What should I watch after Drag Me to Hell?
faq.answer: Try The Ninth Gate, Candyman, Inland Empire—each shares DNA with Drag Me to Hell while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Drag Me to Hell?
faq.answer: Drag Me to Hell (2009) is best described as Horror, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Drag Me to Hell worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this horror lane.
